summary:
The twins in twin-twin transfusion syndrome are genetically identical because the condition occurs solely within monochorionic pregnancies resulting from the split of a single zygote. This inherent origin ensures complete genomic similarity between the coexisting fetuses.

Only include information directly related to the medical/clinical aspects of the query—no extraneous details such as tool calls or document references beyond what’s necessary for supporting claims.

Okay, I need to summarize just the core clinical logic behind the correct answer regarding twin-twin transfusion syndrome and fetal genetics:

Twin-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS) affects only monochorionic twin pairs, which develop from a single zygote through incomplete separation during early embryonic development. Because these twins share a common chorion derived from one original cell line, their genomes are completely identical—a defining feature of monozygous twinning—and thus no instance of TTTS can occur among non-identical (dizygotic) siblings who lack shared placental structure.

This summary captures exactly how the pathophysiology ties into genotype determination without adding irrelevant detail.
